speaker: interviewee
text: OK, well, we arrive in New York. And we arrive on the Sunday night into the harbor. And because it's Sunday, we couldn't dock into the harbor itself, so we were outside the harbor all night. And none of us went to bed-- we partied, we whatever. We were sad, we were happy, we were whatever all night. And we could see the Statue of Liberty. It really was, for me, interesting to see because I've see the other one in Paris on Place de la Republique. We have the original statute, which was donated, you know, to the United States from France. And I saw-- I don't remember the bridge or whatever with cars going by. With the lights, it looked beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So arriving in New York. And yet, when we docked, I was so hesitant, and I let everybody go ahead of me. And I didn't want to get off the ship. And then finally, I said, well, I can go, and if I don't like it, I can always go home again. That was my feeling. And my poor aunt and my girlfriend are waiting for me for about eight hours until I got off the ship. Now I had to walk through a gate where my aunt was standing. And there's this big, black man who's taking my ticket. Why I'm saying that is he spoke Yiddish to me because my aunt told him she's expecting my niece from Paris. So he said to me, is this the [? shayne ?] [? maidel ?] from Paris? My Yiddish is not very well. And I was sort of shocked hearing this person speaking Yiddish. But then I got off in New York, and I went to my aunt's apartment. And then the incident happened. And somebody asked me, tell me, is it really true what we heard about Europe? Is it really-- was it that bad?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just couldn't even answer. And my cousin heard this, and he told him, she's not even going to answer this question. You don't answer a stupid question.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after a while, I realized New York isn't as bad as I thought. The Americans are not quite as bad as I thought it was. And, [INAUDIBLE] before I came to New York, when my mom was in Paris, it was sort of an interesting episode what happened to us. Now I laugh about it. It was really funny because I saw some of these people in New York later. When I was at Chateau de Ferrieres, the Rothschild home, there was a couple there, and they had a little girl. And I was going into Paris at the weekend to be with my family. So they say, would I take their daughter into Paris and to drop them off with some of their relatives? So I said, sure I do. So I took her. And somebody opened a door, and I saw in an apartment after the war that was so unusual. Such a wealthy surrounding. And the gal that opened the door-- I-- I recognized her and she recognized me. Her parents were my parents' closest friends. And they were relatives to this girl, to this little girl. Well, make a long story short, you know, when my mom came, we all got together with them in Paris. And my mother still couldn't figure out how can they amass this fortune? So in meantime, my family, my cousin in New York, used to send us packages, care packages. And I remember she sent me a beautiful coat, but it was red, a red coat. And that was very unusual. In France you didn't wear red coats, but when you're cold, you wear a coat. So I go to visit one afternoon, and somebody opened a door, and it was the police. I mean, today it's funny. And what had happened-- they were dealing in some sort of foreign exchange money. And we were caught in this web.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I tell you, you think of the bad things, you think of the good things. And my mother was very thrilled that she found her old friends back in Paris. But that was such a coincidence with this child, you know, being a relative. But I didn't meet-- not too many people back from Cologne in New York. Not too many. A lot of them just never came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- how did you find the general public's awareness?

speaker: interviewee
text: The general public was very nice. As a matter of fact, I enjoyed it very much. My girlfriend took me, you know, to-- [INAUDIBLE] to tourists, you know, and showed me around. But yet I knew I wasn't going to stay in New York because I would come to Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you destined for Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you why I was destined to Los Angeles-- my sister was here. Also, you remember you heard me talk about the [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were several people that were in New York that I knew. They told me in Paris that they were in New York. And I contacted them. We got together. And they told me that when you take the train to Los Angeles, you stop off in Chicago and you have eight hours in Chicago. And they told me that my girlfriend Angel lives in Chicago. So I said, great.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I left my luggage at the depot, and I hailed a taxi because I don't know Chicago. And I have the address. And I'm sitting in this cab, and I'm driving, and I'm getting scared-- I said, where is this man taking me? And he turned to me and he says, you read Yiddish? Anyway, I lucked out again with a Jewish cab driver. He says, tell me-- do they know you're coming? I said, no, I don't even know where I'm going. He says it's quite a ways. It was in Evanston.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, and he even stayed until somebody answered the door, because he said to me, if there's nobody there, you come back to my house, to my wife, and then I'll take you to the depot tonight. I said, gee, American people are pretty nice people. And I met the aunt, and we went back into city of Chicago, and to meet her. And then she says, I want you to meet a relative of mine. A gentleman is coming for lunch-- a cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said to me, would you do me a favor? When you go to Los Angeles would you call my sister? I have a little gift for her. So after a while, I came here, and I stayed with my sister's foster parents, who, you know, I moved in with. And she said, you know, you better call that Mrs. Jones. And that was my future mother-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how that happened. That was my destiny, to come to Los Angeles. Do you remember the address when you first came to LA?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I lived 101 South Vista Street, Los Angeles 36. It was on Beverly and Vista. You know, Beverly-Fairfax, the area in there? Yeah, that's where I lived. And my foster father was head of cinematography at Fox studio. He started out when he was 16 with cranking the cameras.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sol Halprin-- H-A-L-P-R-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife was Gladys. They were very involved in the Jewish community in Los Angeles, too. They were very well known in the Jewish community. As a matter of fact, she was the original founder of ORT on the west coast.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they had taken care of your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took in my sister and then they took me in. And then I ended up living-- because they were friends-- at the Hamburger home. I don't know if you ever heard of it. It was-- actually, it was owned by the May Company-- the May people that owned the May Company. And the Hamburger home was a home for young Jewish ladies that were working or going to school. You paid there, but it was a very nice milieu, and it was a good place for me to learn English. Because my sister was in school, so it really was a good place for me to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that located?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was on Union near Pico. In those days, that was a pretty decent area yet. And so then I lived there for about a year and I enjoyed it tremendously. And then in 1948, I met my husband. And then '49, we were married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you met him in 1948.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'd met him, yeah, when I first came here, and we were married in '49. And I have a daughter named Georgia Jones-Davis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And her husband's name is Howard. And I have a granddaughter named Emily Hannah Davis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born July 10th, '89. She is six years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was your daughter born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was born April 10th, 1951-- no, April 29th, I'm sorry. My anniversary is April 10th. She's born April 29, 1951. And she is a book editor-- associate book editor-- with the She used to be with the and then with the

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother come out and join you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother came to this country in 1951, shortly after my daughter was born. And she lived with us for a while. And then my husband had a chance to move to Santa Fe. And I really deep down didn't want to go. I was afraid. I didn't want to leave my mother again. And my mother, meantime, was a very adventurous woman. And she met people and made friends. And deep down, she really also wanted to get on her own. And when my husband called and said, would you like to move to Santa Fe, my first impulse was no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then my mother said to me, why did you say no so fast? So I said, well, where are you going to go? She says, don't worry about me. That was the best thing that happened. So we finally moved to Santa Fe, and my mother went on her own. And she used to love to come and visit Santa Fe. It was a wonderful place, and it was a very nice Jewish community there in Santa Fe. How long did you live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We built our own temple. We were there five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name--

speaker: interviewee
text: But we go back all the time. We still have close friends. We go back all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the temple you helped to build?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- you know, I don't remember right now the name of it. I have a picture of it. Besides, they changed it now. They built a brand new temple because they have 250 children in Hebrew school. I mean, that's how Santa Fe Jewish community grew. We also used to go a great deal to Los Alamos when Los Alamos was still a closed city. When you had to go through the Geiger counters and everything else before you could go into the town of Los Alamos. I'm sure you've heard of Los Alamos. So we really had a very nice life in New Mexico, and then ultimately, we came back here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what kind of work did you do? Did you go to work when you were here, or when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Actually, when I came here, I worked for a little while. Yes, I worked for a little while. But then when my husband used to travel on the road a great deal, I used to go with him. I used to travel with him on the road.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my mother wanted me to go, and she wanted to take care of my daughter, so I was always in her way. My husband was in sales in those days. He would be on the road selling. Different things we sold. And then we ended up in the [? Western ?] business. And used to see [? Western ?] stores and clients. We did shows on the road, you know, markets. And so I did that. But then later on, I quit, and I sort of got involved in politics. I joined the Beverly Hills Democratic Women's Club. And we were very active. And we worked for different politicians, which I still try to do today-- not as much as I did then. And now what I do-- then for a while I decided I really am tired of going to luncheon, and I went back to work. What I did-- I worked for people-- for an office where we placed temporary people in positions. I put temporaries and permanent people. It was like a headhunter. And I enjoyed that tremendously. But business got bad, and I got laid off. And then I went to work with my husband. We're in the advertising business. And I thoroughly, for the last three years, worked with him. And the less we want to work, the busier we're getting. And I enjoy that very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any other organizations that you belong to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't, except for the Democratic organization. And I do belong to the Hidden Child Foundation, which-- they're located here, but also mostly in New York. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is that like? What do you-- [? what activities-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, what it is-- it's a group of people that had sort of a background like mine, who were hidden during the war, unlike being in a camp. And our experiences are naturally different than from people that were in the camps. But we felt we're still part of a different world. You know, we kind of were hidden, and then we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we cannot compete or compare, I want to say, with people that actually went in the camp. We had our moments of fear and of tragedies. And then about four, five years ago, we decided-- a group of us decided, the survivors, that we were going to have a reunion in Chateau de Chabannes. And Chateau de Chabannes was the first--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we all went back and met at Chateau de Chabannes. We stayed in a little hotel near there, all of us, for about five days. And then we went back to the Chateau one day. The mayor of the village and the senators and everybody, they kind of opened up the village and the Chateau for us. And it was a lovely place to go back, and everybody-- to get to see everybody. Everybody brought pictures of this, of this, of that. And sadly, there were pictures that I brought back of people that never came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you made it to this? How many survivors of Chateau de Chabannes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were about 50. But there were also some that were in from Europe, from France. We had some came from Israel and from all over the States. From Los Angeles, we were about six from Los Angeles that went--

speaker: interviewer
text: And had you ever--

speaker: interviewee
text: --with their spouses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever gone back to Cologne?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Cologne the first time in 1970 with my husband. My husband had to go on business. And I was very uncomfortable. I showed him where I lived, and after three days, I really felt very uncomfortable being in Germany. Last year-- a year ago-- I was invited to come back, and I didn't go. This year I was invited again, and I decided I do want to go, and I took my daughter. And we went back to-- and I felt a lot more comfortable this time, going back. I met very interesting people. What we had to-- we had to address a school. We spoke to children. High school students asked us questions-- why we left, where me were, what happened from the Holocaust and the whole thing. And that was very interesting. And the students were very receptive. I have to say that. They were eager to learn, very nice, very polite. And we met with the mayor of the city. They wined and dined us. They took us everywhere, even to the opera. And then I met a very interesting woman. She runs a radio program, a call-in show, in Cologne. And I gave her an interview. And why I left. Because there was one incident she wanted to really know. My dad, I told you, was an amateur boxer. He worked with the McCabe Group. I used to see him box, and I enjoyed that. And one day a man came to our door to deliver coal, and it was Max Schmeling. Nobody knew, you know, that's his name. And he saw my dad boxing, and he says, could he come over and kind of work out. He says, yeah. Well, I don't have to tell you, you know, who he ultimately became. And then a very famous boxer, but he didn't know us anymore. And I think it must have been the first and the youngest that was knocked out when I was three years old by Max Schmeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when he came a few years ago to Las Vegas to honor Joe Louis, and my sister said, maybe you should call him. I said I didn't want to call him. I figured no, what's the use? But he used to be very nice. Used to come all the time. So-- Well, in closing would you like to share something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'd like to share my father's last thought. He sent me this letter when he still was in Westerbork, March the 8th, 1943. And he wrote, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That's 'My dear Francis.' [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Which means, 'Keep well, and hopefully we'll see each other real soon. I kiss you and embrace you, thy everloving father.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the last you heard from your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the last I heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to share?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have some pictures if you'd like to see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [? FJ: I brought ?] some pictures. And we're going to bring in your husband, Raymond.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Frances, would you like to introduce this gentleman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This gentleman is my husband. And we've been married for 46 years. I told you we have one daughter and one granddaughter. And he'll tell you the rest.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says we've been married 46 years, and I've always commented that had I taken a shot at her the night we got married, I'd be out on parole now. But I always felt that was the best thing that happened since the invention of sliced bread. This has been a wonderful lady for 46 years. And she's seen me through some bad, rough times when I became ill. We have shared in some experiences during these last 46 years that some were not all that pleasant and some were very pleasant. But she's just been a very great person, and it's been a pleasure for me to be married to her. By the way, my name is Ray. And I know that she has worked hard during the interview today. She's looked forward to doing this, and I appreciate the opportunity to be able to say hello and thank you all very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my mother's star. And in the center, as you see, it says Jude. And she wore that approximately from 1939 on. OK, this is my birth certificate. When I was with the underground, where you had to take a new identity, I became-- I got a new identity and a new birth certificate with my new name, which was Florence Suzanne Van Sant. OK, this is me. This is my identification card. That's a card the underground gave you because you couldn't always carry your birth certificate. And like I say, with the new name, Florence-- Florence Suzanne Van Sant. And I had to carry that in case I was stopped by the French Vichy government or the Germans. I had [? my identity. ?] These are my parents. I don't exactly know what year. Maybe it was 1939 or '40. That was taken at Camp Westerbork in Holland. These are my parents. This was taken in camp in 1939. My mother, Amalia Weinberg, and my father, Josef Weinberg. This is Frances Jones in 1928. I was about four years old. In my favorite little fur coat that my dad had brought me as a present. This is me, Frances Jones. And I must have been about six years old, with my friends. I don't remember their names. It was in Cologne during the-- like in New Orleans, the carnival time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the center one, right in the center. That's Frances Jones. This is me, Frances Jones. I'm the first little girl in front. And this was in 1938, I think, in Cologne. The gentleman in the middle, he's the one that invented this. It was called a foot bicycle. And the young men in back-- I don't remember his name. And I was the only child ever allowed on this because I was such a good cyclist. This is Frances Jones again. I'm on the left. In the center is my husband Ray Jones and my daughter, Georgia Jones-Davis.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was about five or six years ago. [? INT2: 1990? ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Around 1990? Yes, about 1990 it would be. Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, I don't remember where it was taken. We were on vacation someplace. This is my pride and joy. This is Emily Hannah Davis, our only granddaughter. And Emily Hannah Davis goes to Los Encinos school in Encino. And she's six years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was taken two years ago in 1993 or '94 in this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in front of our house-- in her house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: They live on 15000 Greenleaf in Sherman Oaks.
